behaviors.description: Out-of-stock products are excluded from recommendations regardless of relevance score.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is in a single filter predicate — does the availability flag gate the product list correctly? This is one function's logic; no module handoff is needed to expose the bug. The failure appears even if the scoring/ranking pipeline is mocked. REAL-INFRA: None. LLM-DEP: No. The filter either checks the flag or it doesn't.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a product list with mixed in-stock/out-of-stock items spanning a range of relevance scores (including the highest score assigned to an out-of-stock item). Call the availability filter function directly. Assert that the returned list contains zero out-of-stock products and that all in-stock products are present, regardless of score.

behaviors.description: User preference filter excludes product categories the user has explicitly blocked.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is in the preference-filter predicate: given a user's blocked-category list and a product catalog, does the function remove all products in blocked categories? This is self-contained logic with no cross-module handoff needed to expose the failure. REAL-INFRA: None. LLM-DEP: No.
behaviors.test_description: Create a synthetic user preferences object with two blocked categories and a product list containing products in blocked, unblocked, and mixed-category memberships. Invoke the preference filter function directly. Assert that no returned product belongs to a blocked category and that products in unblocked categories are all retained.

behaviors.description: The ranking algorithm scores products matching recent browsing patterns higher than products with no browsing signal.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Verifying that browsing signal actually elevates rank requires the browsing-history loader and the ranking scorer to be wired together; the failure appears at the handoff where browsing data is read and applied to scores. A unit test mocking the history would trivially pass. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the history store hides whether the algorithm actually reads and applies the signal correctly. LLM-DEP: A traditional weighted scorer is deterministic, but if the engine uses an ML ranker the quality claim becomes Agentic — probability reflected below.
behaviors.test_description: Seed a local in-memory browsing-history store with events for product A (high signal) and no events for product B (same base attributes). Run the full recommendation ranking pipeline against both products. Assert that product A receives a higher rank than product B and that the rank difference is attributable to the browsing-signal weight.

behaviors.description: Pagination returns the correct page slice with stable ordering across repeated requests.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Stability failures arise at the boundary between the ranking module (which must produce a deterministically ordered list) and the pagination module (which slices it). A unit test of pagination alone would trivially pass with a fixed input list; the real risk is that the ranked list order varies between calls, making page 2 inconsistent. REAL-INFRA: None needed; a local in-memory data store suffices. LLM-DEP: No.
behaviors.test_description: Populate a local product store with 50 products and a fixed user state. Request page 2 (e.g., items 11–20) twice in sequence with no data changes. Assert that both responses return the same ordered product IDs and that the set is exactly the expected slice of the full ranked list (no overlap with page 1, no duplicates).

behaviors.description: At least one recommendation comes from a category the user has not browsed (serendipity requirement).
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Serendipity enforcement requires the browsing-history module and the diversity/serendipity post-processor to be wired together; the failure only appears when the system actually reads the user's browsed categories and then checks that the final list contains at least one outside them. Mocking history would hide whether category diversity is computed from real signal. LLM-DEP: A rule-based serendipity filter is deterministic; if an ML ranker controls category selection, the quality guarantee becomes Agentic.
behaviors.test_description: Create a user with browsing history covering exactly two categories. Populate a catalog with products in those two categories and products in two additional categories. Run the full recommendation pipeline. Assert that the returned list contains at least one product whose category does not appear in the user's browsing history.

behaviors.description: A new user with no purchase or browsing history receives popularity-based recommendations, not an empty result.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The cold-start failure is a routing bug at the boundary between the history-fetching module (returning empty/null) and the ranking algorithm (which must branch to a popularity fallback instead of returning empty). A unit test that directly calls the ranker with an empty-history argument bypasses the real failure point — whether the pipeline detects the cold-start condition and invokes the fallback at all. REAL-INFRA: None. LLM-DEP: No.
behaviors.test_description: Register a user with no purchase or browsing history in the local history store. Also seed a popularity index with known top-N products. Run the full recommendation pipeline for this user. Assert that (a) the result is non-empty, (b) the returned products match the global popularity rankings, and (c) no personalized signal was applied.

behaviors.description: Click tracking records the correct product ID, position in the recommendation list, and timestamp.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: 'Position in the list' is derived from the recommendation list, not from the click event alone — the tracking handler must receive or compute position relative to the rendered result set. The failure appears at the boundary where the recommendation list is passed to (or accessible by) the tracking module. A unit test of the tracking function with a hard-coded position would not catch a bug where position is mis-calculated from the list. REAL-INFRA: Local in-memory or filesystem event store suffices. LLM-DEP: No.
behaviors.test_description: Generate a recommendation list of 10 products using the local pipeline with a seeded data store. Simulate a click on the product at position 4 (0-indexed). Call the click-tracking module with the recommendation context. Read back the tracking record and assert that product_id, position (4), and timestamp (within a small tolerance) are all recorded correctly.

behaviors.description: Recommendation response time stays under 200ms for a catalog of 100,000 products.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Performance depends on real computation and I/O over real data volume; any mock of the catalog trivially eliminates the latency. REAL-INFRA: Requires a real data store populated with 100K products, real network or subprocess calls, and real CPU/memory allocation. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the catalog would make this test meaningless — it would always pass. LLM-DEP: No. The 200ms SLA is only observable under realistic infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Seed a real (or containerized) product database with 100,000 product records reflecting realistic field cardinality. Issue a recommendation request for a user with a representative history profile. Measure wall-clock latency from request dispatch to response received (including all filtering, ranking, and pagination). Assert the measured latency is below 200ms across at least 10 consecutive runs (to rule out cold-start outliers).

behaviors.description: A product purchased by the user is deprioritized in subsequent recommendation requests.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure is at the boundary between the purchase-event recorder and the ranking algorithm: the purchase must be persisted, then read back and applied as a deprioritization signal in the next ranking call. A unit test of the ranker with a hard-coded purchase list bypasses the real failure point — whether the pipeline actually reads and applies the purchase record on a subsequent call. REAL-INFRA: Local in-memory store suffices. LLM-DEP: Deprioritization may be rule-based (deterministic) or ML-influenced; probability reflected below.
behaviors.test_description: Generate an initial recommendation list for a user and note the rank of product X. Record a purchase event for product X in the local purchase store. Generate a second recommendation list for the same user with unchanged catalog data. Assert that product X's rank is lower in the second list than in the first (or that it is absent if fully suppressed), and that no other product's relative order has changed due to the purchase signal.

behaviors.description: Recommendations are deterministic — two requests with identical user state return the same ranked list.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: Non-determinism in the full pipeline is more likely to originate from hash-map ordering, floating-point tiebreaking, or thread scheduling across modules than within a single function. A unit test of the ranker in isolation with a fixed input will always be deterministic; the failure only surfaces when the full pipeline (history fetch → scoring → sort → paginate) is exercised end-to-end. REAL-INFRA: No external infrastructure needed. LLM-DEP: If the ranker uses an LLM/ML model, determinism cannot be guaranteed — but the behavior explicitly requires it, making Agentic the level if ML is involved.
behaviors.test_description: Fix a user state (browsing history, preferences, purchase history) in the local in-memory stores. Call the full recommendation pipeline twice in sequence with no intervening mutations. Assert that both responses return identical product ID sequences in identical order and that all metadata (scores, page info) matches exactly.
